Another thing is low precision training. So low precision training is something that's necessary, where do you're training very large models or large large ish models on pure GPUs.
So training it full 32 bit precision can be costly. So it's pretty common to train at, for example, 16 bit precision, especially if you're training all of the parameters of the models.
And there's kind of two alternatives for this.
First one is FT 16 and that's the standard 16 bet floating point numbers that are used by most computers and most CPUs.
And these floating point numbers, they allocate one bit for the sign, five bits for the exponent and 10 bits for the fractional components.
And relatively precise fractions and.
Exponents with a relatively small range, so they can't express things with very large or very small exponents.
There's something called beef float 16 in beef float 16 B stands for brain for like Google brain.
So there's two different methods for that.
So you can see that there's two different methods for that.
And so that's where they invented this. And basically what it does is it increases the number of bits.
For the exponent to eight. And it decreases the number of bits for the fraction to seven.
So that allows you to express a wider range of values at a lower precision, essentially.
And this is much, much more stable with respect to training because you can handle very small numbers and very large numbers better.
So this is pretty essential and I would recommend that no matter what you're doing if you're doing 16 bet you would use this.

Another thing that you should be aware of, especially if you're training very large models is check pointing and resets.
So even through best efforts training can go south that can have problems. So what do you do?
The first thing that you can do is you can monitor for possible issues.

And then after it's spiked, you can see that the complexity of the model is going down after the spike continues to go down for a little bit, but then it starts going up.
And so basically once it started going up now your model is kind of in like a bad space. It's in a bad space of the parameter space.
It will just continue being in a bad space until it diverges. But it's hard to diagnose immediately other than through things like the gradient norm. So monitoring the gradient norm can be helpful.
This is especially important if you're training very large. If you're training smaller models, it's not as big a problem.
But this is something to pay attention to.
If training crashes, what can you do? So a very common thing to do is to roll back to a previous checkpoint like save out checkpoints periodically.

Roll back not to write before the gradient spike, but roll back to like 100 steps before the gradient spike shuffle your training data set or jump to a different part of your training data set and resume.
